questions.questionText: The first vascular response to an acute injury is:
questions.explanation: Brief neurogenic vasoconstriction is immediately followed by characteristic vasodilation.

questions.questionText: The predominant inflammatory cell in the first 6-24 hours of acute inflammation is the:
questions.explanation: Neutrophils are the first responders, later replaced by monocytes/macrophages.

questions.questionText: Which mediator is primarily responsible for the pain of acute inflammation?
questions.explanation: Bradykinin and PGE2 sensitive nerve endings to cause pain.

questions.questionText: Opsonization of bacteria to enhance phagocytosis is primarily mediated by:
questions.explanation: C3b and IgG act as powerful opsonins.

questions.questionText: Which selectin is expressed on endothelial cells and mediates early leukocyte rolling?
questions.explanation: Endothelial (E) and Platelet (P) selectins mediate leukocyte rolling.

questions.questionText: Firm adhesion of leukocytes to the endothelium is mediated primarily by:
questions.explanation: Integrins (LFA-1, MAC-1) strongly bind ICAMs during firm adhesion.

questions.questionText: The hallmark histological feature of granulomatous inflammation is the presence of:
questions.explanation: Aggregates of activated macrophages resembling epithelial cells are required for the diagnosis of a granuloma.

questions.questionText: Which cytokine is crucial for maintaining granuloma formation in tuberculosis?
questions.explanation: Th1 cells secrete IFN-y to continuously activate macrophages.

questions.questionText: A scar formed from excessive accumulation of collagen that grows beyond the boundaries of the original wound is a:
questions.explanation: Unlike hypertrophic scars, keloids do not regress and expand beyond the injury site.

questions.questionText: Type I collagen is replacing Type III collagen during which phase of wound healing?
questions.explanation: Matrix metalloproteinases degrade Type III to be replaced by stronger Type I during remodeling.

questions.questionText: In Inflammation, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Pathology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Pathology references for Inflammation. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Inflammation.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Inflammation scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Inflammation.
